title: Citrus vinegar all-purpose cleaner
subtitle: A spray cleaner made from citrus peel infused white vinegar, diluted with water
excerpt: A spray cleaner made by steeping orange or lemon peel in white vinegar for two weeks, then diluting with water in a spray bottle. The peel limonene cuts grease; the vinegar dissolves limescale; the dilution makes the spray safe for most kitchen surfaces. Skip on stone, marble, and waxed wood.
sourceNotes: Citrus-peel-in-vinegar is a long-standing household cleaning recipe. The limonene from the peel acts as a mild grease cutter; the acetic acid dissolves mineral scale. 50:50 dilution with water is the standard ratio for an everyday spray; 1:1 undiluted for tougher limescale.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 x 500 ml spray bottle ready cleaner + 250 ml infused vinegar concentrate kept in a jar
recipe.batchNotes: The infusion scales by the kilner jar size. A 1 L jar takes about 8 orange or lemon peels and 700 ml of vinegar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Steep the citrus peel in vinegar for 2 weeks. Save peels in a jar in the fridge as you eat the fruit; top up the vinegar as the peels come.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: The strained vinegar concentrate keeps 12 months in a lidded jar. The diluted spray keeps 3 months at room temperature; the dilution reduces the preservation strength of the vinegar. Discard if the spray smells musty or develops floating particles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Citrus peels contain d-limonene in the oil glands of the rind, at roughly 90% concentration in the peel oil. Limonene is a mild solvent that cuts greasy films - the principle behind orange-oil commercial degreasers. Steeping peel in vinegar for 2 weeks extracts a small fraction of the limonene into the vinegar, enough to boost grease-cutting noticeably.

Acetic acid dissolves limescale and is safe on glass, stainless steel, sealed laminate, ceramic tile, plastic, glazed earthenware. Skip on stone (marble, granite, limestone, slate), waxed wood, polished brass, copper, iron, and natural sealed wood floors - the acid etches the surface or strips the seal. Test a hidden corner if unsure.

content.text: Citrus peel and vinegar make a cleaner that punches above its three-ingredient list. The vinegar dissolves limescale on the kitchen sink and shower screen; the
extracted from the peel boosts the grease-cutting on hob splashes and oven-door grime. The spray smells of orange or lemon rather than vinegar once diluted.

are firm: safe on glass, sealed laminate, stainless steel, ceramic tile, plastic, glazed earthenware. Skip on marble, granite, limestone, slate, waxed wood, brass, copper, and any porous stone. Test a hidden corner if unsure.

items.prepNote: Standard 5% acetic acid distilled white vinegar from the supermarket. Stronger pickling vinegar (10%) gives a more aggressive cleaner; dilute the spray more heavily if using.
Peel from 4 unwaxed lemons OR 3 oranges OR a mix. Use only the coloured zest plus a small amount of pith; remove flesh entirely. Saves the peels from eating-fruit.
Tap water or distilled. The dilution that makes the cleaner everyday-safe.

content.text: Pack the citrus peel into a clean dry 1 L kilner jar. Pour the white vinegar over until the peels are fully submerged - top with a small saucer if the peels float clear of the vinegar. Clip the jar shut and date the lid. Stand somewhere out of direct sunlight for 14 days. The vinegar will turn pale gold (lemon) or pale orange (orange) as the peel oils diffuse out.

Strain the infused vinegar through a fine sieve into a clean measuring jug, pressing the peels gently to release. Compost the peels. The strained concentrate keeps 12 months in a lidded jar. For the spray, dilute one part concentrate to one part water (250 ml concentrate + 250 ml water). Funnel into a 500 ml spray bottle. Label clearly - this looks like water in the bottle, so a hand-written label saves a wrong-bottle slip.

Spray onto the surface, leave 30 seconds, wipe with a damp cloth or microfibre. Buff dry on shiny surfaces. For limescale on a shower screen, spray more heavily and leave 5 minutes before wiping. For greasy hob splashes, spray and wipe twice. Do not use on stone, marble, waxed wood, brass, copper, or polished untreated wood.

Strained concentrate in a lidded jar lasts 12 months. The diluted spray lasts 3 months in the spray bottle. Refresh from concentrate when the spray runs low. Discard if either smells musty rather than clean-vinegar-and-citrus.
